message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  The COVID-19 pandemic has undergone frequent and rapid changes in its local and global infection rates, driven by governmental measures, or the emergence of new viral variants. The reproduction number
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  indicates the average number of cases generated by an infected person at time
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  and is a key indicator of the spread of an epidemic. A timely estimation of
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is a crucial tool to enable governmental organizations to adapt quickly to these changes and assess the consequences of their policies. The EpiEstim method is the most widely accepted method for estimating
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . But it estimates
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  with a significant temporal delay. Here, we propose a new method,
                  <jats:italic>EpiInvert</jats:italic>
                  , that shows good agreement with EpiEstim, but that provides estimates of
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  several days in advance. We show that
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  can be estimated by inverting the renewal equation linking
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  with the observed incidence curve of new cases,
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . Our signal processing approach to this problem yields both
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and a restored
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  corrected for the “weekend effect” by applying a deconvolution + denoising procedure. The implementations of the EpiInvert and EpiEstim methods are fully open-source and can be run in real-time on every country in the world, and every US state through a web interface at
                  <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://www.ipol.im/epiinvert">www.ipol.im/epiinvert</jats:ext-link>
                  .
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Significance Statement</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Based on a signal processing approach we propose a method to compute the reproduction number
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    , the transmission potential of an epidemic over time.
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    is estimated by minimizing a functional that enforces: (i) the ability to produce an incidence curve
                    <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    corrected of the weekly periodic bias produced by the “weekend effect”, obtained from
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    through a renewal equation; (ii) the regularity of
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . A good agreement is found between our
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    estimate and the one provided by the currently accepted method, EpiEstim, except our method predicts
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    several days closer to present. We provide the mathematical arguments for this shift. Both methods, applied every day on each country, can be compared at
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://www.ipol.im/epiinvert">www.ipol.im/epiinvert</jats:ext-link>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Computing the daily reproduction number of COVID-19 by inverting the renewal equation using a variational technique

reference.article-title: A new framework and software to estimate time-varying reproduction numbers during epidemics

reference.unstructured: H Nishiura , G Chowell , The Effective Reproduction Number as a Prelude to Statistical Estimation of Time-Dependent Epidemic Trends, eds. G Chowell , JM Hyman , LMA Bettencourt , C Castillo-Chavez . (Springer Netherlands, Dordrecht), pp. 103–121 (2009).
K Gostic , et al., Practical considerations for measuring the effective reproductive number, Rt. MedRxiv (2020).

reference.article-title: Practical considerations for measuring the effective reproductive number, rt
The r0 package: a toolbox to estimate reproduction numbers for epidemic outbreaks
A Daily Measure of the SARS-CoV-2 Effective Reproduction Number for all Countries

reference.unstructured: S Ma , et al., Epidemiological parameters of coronavirus disease 2019: a pooled analysis of publicly reported individual data of 1155 cases from seven countries. Medrxiv (2020).
J Demongeot , K Oshinubi , H Seligmann , F Thuderoz , Estimation of daily reproduction rates in covid-19 outbreak. medRxiv (2021).

reference.article-title: Modern regularization methods for inverse problems
Forecasting ebola with a regression transmission model
reference.author: The RAPIDD Ebola Forecasting Challenge

reference.unstructured: Z Du , et al., The serial interval of COVID-19 from publicly reported confirmed cases. medRxiv (2020).
H Nishiura , NM Linton , AR Akhmetzhanov , Serial interval of novel coronavirus (COVID-19) infections. Int. journal infectious diseases (2020).
SW Park , et al., Forward-looking serial intervals correctly link epidemic growth to reproduction numbers. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 118 (2021).
NG Davies , et al., Estimated transmissibility and impact of sars-cov-2 lineage b.1.1.7 in england. Science 372 (2021).
L Alvarez , Comparative analysis of the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic in South Korea, Italy, Spain, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the USA and the New-York state. MedRxiv (2020).
